title: Building Performant Real-Time Dashboards with Next.js and D3
excerpt: How I built a financial dashboard that handles 10K+ data points with smooth 60fps animations using Next.js, D3.js, and WebSocket connections.
body:
The Challenge

When a fintech startup approached me to build their real-time dashboard, the requirements were clear: display thousands of data points updating every second, with smooth animations and zero jank.

Architecture Decisions

I chose Next.js for the framework, D3.js for data visualization, and WebSocket for real-time data streaming. The key insight was to separate the data layer from the rendering layer.

Data Layer

WebSocket connections feed into a shared state store using Zustand. This keeps all components in sync without prop drilling or context re-renders.

Rendering Layer

D3 handles the heavy lifting of SVG manipulation. Instead of letting React reconcile SVG elements (which is slow for thousands of nodes), I use D3's enter/update/exit pattern inside useEffect hooks.

Performance Tricks

  • Canvas fallback — For datasets over 5K points, I switch from SVG to Canvas rendering
  • Web Workers — Heavy calculations (moving averages, percentiles) run off the main thread
  • Virtual scrolling — Table views only render visible rows
  • Debounced resize — Chart redraws are debounced to prevent layout thrashing

Results

The final dashboard maintains 60fps with 10K+ data points updating in real-time. Lighthouse performance score: 96. Time to interactive: under 2 seconds.
